title: Von dem Fischer un syner Fru
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 19. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly. This tale is in Low German (Plattdeutsch); the glossary gives the High German equivalent for each dialect form.
about: KHM 19, and one of the darkest tales in the Grimms’ collection: a fisherman frees an enchanted flounder, and his wife Ilsebill sends him back to it again and again — for a cottage, a stone castle, a kingship, an empire, the papacy, and at last to be like God — until the sea, angrier at every wish, casts them back into their filthy Pißputt (hovel) where they began. Unlike most tales in the collection, this one is written entirely in Low German (Plattdeutsch), the North-German dialect the Grimms recorded it in, so almost every word differs from High German. You will meet dialect forms such as un (und), syner (seiner), Fru (Frau), wat (was), dat (das), de (die), ik (ich), wull (wollte), Butt (flounder), and the refrain’s Manntje and Buttje.

sentences.t: Dar wöör maal eens en Fischer un syne Fru, de waanden tosamen in’n Pißputt, dicht an der See, un de Fischer güng alle Dage hen un angeld: un he angeld un angeld.
sentences.l: There was once upon a time a fisherman and his wife, who dwelt together in a pisspot, close by the sea, and the fisherman went out every day and angled: and he angled and angled.
sentences.i: There was once a fisherman and his wife, who lived together in a hovel close by the sea; and the fisherman went out every day and fished, and he fished and fished.
sentences.n: This whole tale is in Low German (Plattdeutsch), the North-German dialect the Grimms took it down in — not the High German of the other tales — so almost every word looks a little foreign. Some key equivalents here: wöör = High German ‘wurde/war’, maal = ‘mal’, en = ‘ein’, un = ‘und’, syne = ‘seine’, Fru = ‘Frau’, de = ‘die’, waanden = ‘wohnten’, tosamen = ‘zusammen’, güng = ‘ging’, Dage = ‘Tage’, hen = ‘hin’. A Pißputt is literally a ‘piss-pot’ — a filthy hovel; the tale ends exactly where it begins, back in the Pißputt.

sentences.t: So seet he ook eens by de Angel un seeg jümmer in dat blanke Water henin: un he seet un seet.
sentences.l: So he too sat once by the fishing-line and looked ever into the bright water in: and he sat and sat.
sentences.i: So once he sat by his line and gazed ever into the clear water, and he sat and sat.
sentences.n: seet = High German ‘saß’ (sat); seeg = ‘sah’ (saw); dat = ‘das’; blanke = ‘blanke’ (bright, shining); henin = ‘hinein’. The little word jümmer (= ‘immer’, always/ever) recurs throughout the tale.

sentences.t: Do güng de Angel to Grund, deep ünner, un as he se heruphaald, so haald he enen grooten Butt heruut.
sentences.l: Then the line went to the bottom, deep under, and as he hauled it up, so he hauled out a great flounder.
sentences.i: Then the line went down to the bottom, deep under, and when he hauled it up, he hauled out a great flounder.
sentences.n: The Butt is the flatfish, a flounder (High German ‘Butt’) — and the enchanted prince of this tale. haald/heruphaald = ‘holte/heraufholte’; enen = ‘einen’; grooten = ‘großen’; heruut = ‘heraus’; ünner = ‘unter’.

sentences.t: Do säd de Butt to em „hör mal, Fischer, ik bidd dy, laat my lewen, ik bün keen rechten Butt, ik bün’n verwünschten Prins.
sentences.l: Then the flounder said to him, “hear now, fisherman, I pray you, let me live, I am no proper flounder, I am an enchanted prince.
sentences.i: Then the flounder said to him, “Listen, fisherman, I pray you, let me live; I am no true flounder, I am an enchanted prince.
sentences.n: säd = High German ‘sagte’; ik = ‘ich’; bidd = ‘bitte’; dy = ‘dir/dich’; laat = ‘lass’; my = ‘mir/mich’; lewen = ‘leben’; bün = ‘bin’; verwünschten = ‘verwünschten’ (enchanted). Note ik bün’n = ik bün en, ‘ich bin ein.’

sentences.t: Wat helpt dy dat, dat du my doot maakst?
sentences.l: What does it help you, that you make me dead?
sentences.i: What good will it do you to kill me?
sentences.n: wat = High German ‘was’; doot = ‘tot’ (dead); maakst = ‘machst.’ ‘doot maken’ = to kill (literally to make dead).

sentences.t: ik würr dy doch nich recht smecken: sett my wedder in dat Water un laat my swemmen.“
sentences.l: I would surely not taste right to you: set me back into the water and let me swim.”
sentences.i: I should not taste right to you anyway; put me back into the water and let me swim.”
sentences.n: würr = High German ‘würde’; nich = ‘nicht’; smecken = ‘schmecken’; sett = ‘setz’; wedder = ‘wieder’; swemmen = ‘schwimmen.’

sentences.t: „Nu,“ säd de Mann, „du bruukst nich so veel Wöörd to maken, eenen Butt, de spreken kann, hadd ik doch wol swemmen laten.“
sentences.l: “Now,” said the man, “you need not make so many words; a flounder that can speak, I would surely have let swim anyhow.”
sentences.i: “Well,” said the man, “you needn’t make so many words about it; a flounder that can talk, I’d have let swim in any case.”
sentences.n: bruukst = High German ‘brauchst’; veel = ‘viel’; Wöörd = ‘Worte’ (words); eenen = ‘einen’; spreken = ‘sprechen’; wol = ‘wohl’; laten = ‘lassen.’

sentences.t: Mit des sett’t he em wedder in dat blanke Water, do güng de Butt to Grund un leet enen langen Strypen Bloot achter sik.
sentences.l: With that he set him back into the bright water, then the flounder went to the bottom and left a long streak of blood behind him.
sentences.i: With that he put it back into the clear water, and the flounder went down to the bottom, leaving a long streak of blood behind it.
sentences.n: ‘Mit des’ = High German ‘damit / mit dem’ (with that). leet = ‘ließ’; Strypen = ‘Streifen’ (streak); Bloot = ‘Blut’; achter = ‘hinter’ (behind); sik = ‘sich.’

sentences.t: Do stünn de Fischer up un güng na syne Fru in’n Pißputt.
sentences.l: Then the fisherman stood up and went to his wife in the pisspot.
sentences.i: Then the fisherman got up and went home to his wife in the hovel.
sentences.n: stünn = High German ‘stand’; up = ‘auf’; na = ‘nach/zu’ (to); in’n = ‘in den.’

sentences.t: „Mann,“ säd de Fru, „hest du hüüt niks fungen?“
sentences.l: “Man,” said the wife, “have you caught nothing today?”
sentences.i: “Husband,” said the wife, “have you caught nothing today?”
sentences.n: hest = High German ‘hast’; hüüt = ‘heute’; niks = ‘nichts’; fungen = ‘gefangen’ (caught).

sentences.t: „Ne,“ säd de Mann, „ik füng enen Butt, de säd he wöör en verwünschten Prins, do hebb ik em wedder swemmen laten.“
sentences.l: “No,” said the man, “I caught a flounder, who said he was an enchanted prince, so I have let him swim again.”
sentences.i: “No,” said the man, “I caught a flounder who said he was an enchanted prince, so I let him swim off again.”
sentences.n: ne = ‘nein’; füng = High German ‘fing’ (caught); hebb = ‘habe.’

sentences.t: „Hest du dy denn niks wünschd?“ säd de Fru.
sentences.l: “Have you then wished yourself nothing?” said the wife.
sentences.i: “Then did you wish nothing for yourself?” said the wife.
sentences.n: wünschd = High German ‘gewünscht’; denn = ‘denn.’

sentences.t: „Ne,“ säd de Mann, „wat schull ik my wünschen?“
sentences.l: “No,” said the man, “what should I wish myself?”
sentences.i: “No,” said the man, “what should I wish for?”
sentences.n: schull = High German ‘sollte’ (should).

sentences.t: „Ach,“ säd de Fru, „dat is doch äwel, hyr man jümmer in’n Pißputt to waanen, dat stinkt un is so eeklig: du haddst uns doch ene lüttje Hütt wünschen kunnt.
sentences.l: “Oh,” said the wife, “it is surely wretched, to dwell here always in the pisspot, it stinks and is so disgusting: you could surely have wished us a little cottage.
sentences.i: “Oh,” said the wife, “it is a wretched thing to live here forever in this hovel, it stinks and is so foul; you might surely have wished us a little cottage.
sentences.n: äwel = High German ‘übel’ (wretched, bad); hyr = ‘hier’; man = ‘nur’ (just, only — a very common Low German particle, NOT ‘man’); waanen/wanen = ‘wohnen’ (to dwell); eeklig = ‘eklig’ (disgusting); haddst = ‘hättest’; ene = ‘eine’; lüttje = ‘kleine’ (little — one of the commonest LG words); Hütt = ‘Hütte’ (cottage); kunnt = ‘gekonnt/könnt.’

sentences.t: Ga noch hen un roop em: segg em wy wählt ’ne lüttje Hütt hebben, he dait dat gewiß.
sentences.l: Go once more thither and call him: tell him we want to have a little cottage, he surely does it.
sentences.i: Go again and call him; tell him we would like a little cottage, he will surely do it.
sentences.n: ga = High German ‘geh’; roop = ‘ruf’; segg = ‘sag’; wy = ‘wir’; wählt = here ‘wollen’ (want — beware: NOT ‘wählen’, to choose); hebben = ‘haben’; dait = ‘tut’ (does); gewiß = ‘gewiss’ (surely).

sentences.t: „Ach,“ säd de Mann, „wat schull ick door noch hengaan?“
sentences.l: “Oh,” said the man, “what should I go there again for?”
sentences.i: “Oh,” said the man, “why should I go there again?”
sentences.n: ick = High German ‘ich’ (a variant of ik); door = ‘da/dort’ (there); hengaan = ‘hingehen.’

sentences.t: „I,“ säd de Fru, „du haddst em doch fungen, un hest em wedder swemmen laten, he dait dat gewiß.
sentences.l: “Eh,” said the wife, “you had surely caught him, and let him swim again, he surely does it.
sentences.i: “Why,” said the wife, “you did catch him, and let him swim away again; he will surely do it.
sentences.n: ‘I’ is a dialect interjection, roughly ‘why!, eh!’

sentences.t: Ga glyk hen.“
sentences.l: Go right away thither.”
sentences.i: Go at once.”
sentences.n: glyk = High German ‘gleich’ (at once).

sentences.t: De Mann wull noch nich recht, wull awerst syn Fru ook nich to weddern syn un güng hen na der See.
sentences.l: The man would still not rightly, but would also not be against his wife, and went thither to the sea.
sentences.i: The man was still not really willing, but would not go against his wife either, and went off to the sea.
sentences.n: wull = High German ‘wollte’ (wanted); awerst = ‘aber’ (but); ook = ‘auch’ (also); ‘to weddern syn’ = to be contrary/against (weddern ≈ ‘zuwider/widern’).

sentences.t: As he door köhm, wöör de See ganß gröön un geel un goor nich meer so blank.
sentences.l: As he came there, the sea was all green and yellow and quite not so bright any more.
sentences.i: When he came there, the sea was all green and yellow and no longer so clear.
sentences.n: köhm = High German ‘kam’; ganß = ‘ganz’; gröön = ‘grün’ (green); geel = ‘gelb’ (yellow); goor = ‘gar’; meer = ‘mehr.’ Watch the sea: it tracks the wife’s greed, growing angrier at every wish — here green and yellow, and later violet, black, and at last a full storm.

sentences.t: So güng he staan un säd
sentences.l: So he went and stood and said
sentences.i: So he took his stand and said:
sentences.n: ‘güng ... staan’ = went and stood (High German ‘ging stehen’); staan = ‘stehen.’

sentences.t:
„Manntje, Manntje, Timpe Te,
Buttje, Buttje in der See,
myne Fru de Ilsebill
will nich so as ik wol will.“
sentences.l:
“Mannikin, mannikin, Timpe Te,
Flounder, flounder in the sea,
my wife the Ilsebill
will not as I would will.”
sentences.i:
“Little man, little man, Timpe Te,
Flounder, flounder in the sea,
my wife, my Ilsebill,
wills not as I would will.”
sentences.n: The verse refrain, which returns after every wish (angrier each time). High German for the key words: Manntje = ‘Männchen’ (little man — the flounder’s name for the fisherman); Timpe Te is a nonsense rhyme-tag (Timpe ≈ a corner or tip); Buttje = ‘Büttchen’, the diminutive of Butt (flounder); myne = ‘meine’; Fru = ‘Frau’; Ilsebill is the wife’s name; as = ‘wie/als’ (as); wol = ‘wohl.’ The sense: ‘my wife Ilsebill will not what I would want.’

sentences.t: Do köhm de Butt answemmen un säd „na, wat will se denn?“
sentences.l: Then the flounder came swimming up and said, “well, what does she want then?”
sentences.i: Then the flounder came swimming up and said, “Well, what does she want, then?”
sentences.n: answemmen = High German ‘anschwimmen’ (to come swimming up); se = ‘sie’ (she).

sentences.t: „Ach,“ säd de Mann, „ik hebb dy doch fungen hatt, nu säd myn Fru ik hadd my doch wat wünschen schullt.
sentences.l: “Oh,” said the man, “I did catch you after all, now my wife said I should surely have wished myself something.
sentences.i: “Oh,” said the man, “I did catch you; now my wife says I really ought to have wished for something.
sentences.n: ‘fungen hatt’ = ‘gefangen gehabt’; schullt = High German ‘gesollt’ (past participle of ‘sollen’, ought).

sentences.t: Se mag nich meer in’n Pißputt wanen, se wull geern ’ne Hütt.“
sentences.l: She wants no more to dwell in the pisspot, she would gladly a cottage.”
sentences.i: She no longer wants to live in the hovel; she would like a cottage.”
sentences.n: mag = High German ‘mag’ (wants, likes); geern = ‘gern(e)’ (gladly).

sentences.t: „Ga man hen,“ säd de Butt, „se hett se all.“
sentences.l: “Just go thither,” said the flounder, “she has it already.”
sentences.i: “Just go home,” said the flounder, “she has it already.”
sentences.n: ‘ga man hen’ = just go along (man = ‘nur’, just); hett = High German ‘hat’ (has); ‘se hett se all’ — the second se = ‘sie’ (it, the cottage); all = ‘schon’ (already).

sentences.t: Do güng de Mann hen, un syne Fru seet nich meer in’n Pißputt, dar stünn awerst ene lüttje Hütt, un syne Fru seet vor de Döhr up ene Bänk.
sentences.l: Then the man went thither, and his wife sat no more in the pisspot; there stood however a little cottage, and his wife sat before the door on a bench.
sentences.i: Then the man went home, and his wife no longer sat in the hovel; instead there stood a little cottage, and his wife sat before the door on a bench.
sentences.n: dar = High German ‘da’ (there); Döhr = ‘Tür’ (door); Bänk = ‘Bank’ (bench).

sentences.t: Do nöhm syne Fru em by de Hand un säd to em „kumm man herin, süh, nu is dat doch veel beter.“
sentences.l: Then his wife took him by the hand and said to him, “just come in, look, now it is surely much better.”
sentences.i: Then his wife took him by the hand and said to him, “Just come in; see, now it is much better.”
sentences.n: nöhm = High German ‘nahm’ (took); kumm = ‘komm’; herin = ‘herein’; süh = ‘sieh’ (look); beter = ‘besser’ (better).

sentences.t: Do güngen se henin, un in de Hütt was een lüttjen Vörplatz un ene lüttje herrliche Stuw un Kamer, wo jem eer Bedd stünn, un Kääk un Spysekamer, allens up dat beste mit Gerädschoppen, un up dat schönnste upgefleyt, Tinntüüg un Mischen (Messing), wat sik darin höört.
sentences.l: Then they went in, and in the cottage was a little entrance-hall and a splendid little parlour and chamber, where their bed stood, and kitchen and larder, everything of the best with utensils, and adorned in the finest way, pewter-ware and brass, whatever belongs therein.
sentences.i: Then they went in, and in the cottage there was a little entrance-hall and a fine little parlour and bedchamber, where their bed stood, and a kitchen and larder, everything fitted out with the best of utensils, and set out in the handsomest way, pewter and brass, all that belongs in such a place.
sentences.n: güngen = High German ‘gingen’; Vörplatz = ‘Vorplatz’ (entrance-hall); Stuw = ‘Stube’ (parlour); Kamer = ‘Kammer’ (chamber); ‘jem eer’ = ‘ihnen ihr’ = their; Kääk = ‘Küche’ (kitchen); Spysekamer = ‘Speisekammer’ (larder); allens = ‘alles’; Gerädschoppen = ‘Gerätschaften’ (utensils); upgefleyt ≈ ‘aufgeputzt’ (adorned); Tinntüüg = ‘Zinnzeug’ (pewter-ware); höört = ‘gehört’ (belongs). The ‘(Messing)’ is the Grimms’ own gloss of the dialect word Mischen (brass).

sentences.t: Un achter was ook en lüttjen Hof mit Hönern un Aanten, un en lüttjen Goorn mit Grönigkeiten un Aaft (Obst).
sentences.l: And behind was also a little yard with hens and ducks, and a little garden with green-stuff and fruit.
sentences.i: And behind there was also a little yard with hens and ducks, and a little garden with greens and fruit.
sentences.n: achter = High German ‘hinten/dahinter’ (behind); Hof = ‘Hof’ (yard); Hönern = ‘Hühnern’ (hens); Aanten = ‘Enten’ (ducks); Goorn = ‘Garten’ (garden); Grönigkeiten = ‘Grünzeug’ (greens); Aaft = ‘Obst’ (fruit — glossed in the source).

sentences.t: „Süh,“ säd de Fru, „is dat nich nett?“
sentences.l: “Look,” said the wife, “is that not nice?”
sentences.i: “See,” said the wife, “isn’t that nice?”
sentences.n: nett = High German ‘nett’ (nice).

sentences.t: „Ja,“ säd de Mann, „so schall’t blywen, nu wähl wy recht vergnöögt lewen.“
sentences.l: “Yes,” said the man, “so shall it stay, now we will live right merrily.”
sentences.i: “Yes,” said the man, “so it shall stay; now we’ll live right merrily.”
sentences.n: schall = High German ‘soll’ (shall); blywen = ‘bleiben’ (to stay); wähl = ‘wollen’ (will/want — again NOT ‘wählen’, to choose); vergnöögt = ‘vergnügt’ (merry); lewen = ‘leben.’

sentences.t: „Dat wähl wy uns bedenken“ säd de Fru.
sentences.l: “That we will think over,” said the wife.
sentences.i: “That we shall think over,” said the wife.
sentences.n: ‘sik bedenken’ = to think over, consider; wähl = ‘wollen.’

sentences.t: Mit des eeten se wat un güngen to Bedd.
sentences.l: With that they ate something and went to bed.
sentences.n: eeten = High German ‘aßen’ (ate).

sentences.t: So güng dat wol ’n acht oder veertein Dag, do säd de Fru „hör, Mann, de Hütt is ook goor to eng, un de Hof un de Goorn is so kleen: de Butt hadd uns ook wol een grötter Huus schenken kunnt.
sentences.l: So it went on surely some eight or fourteen days, then the wife said, “hear, man, the cottage is after all too cramped, and the yard and the garden is so small: the flounder could surely have given us a bigger house as well.
sentences.i: So it went on for a week or a fortnight, and then the wife said, “Listen, husband, the cottage is far too cramped, and the yard and garden are so small; the flounder could well have given us a bigger house.
sentences.n: veertein = High German ‘vierzehn’ (fourteen); Dag = ‘Tag(e)’; eng = ‘eng’ (cramped); kleen = ‘klein’ (small); grötter = ‘größer’ (bigger); Huus = ‘Haus’; schenken = ‘schenken’ (to give).

sentences.t: Ik much woll in enem grooten stenern Slott wanen: ga hen tom Butt, he schall uns en Slott schenken.“
sentences.l: I would gladly dwell in a great stone castle: go thither to the flounder, he shall give us a castle.”
sentences.i: I should like to live in a great stone castle; go to the flounder, he shall give us a castle.”
sentences.n: much = High German ‘möchte’ (would like); enem = ‘einem’; stenern = ‘steinern’ (of stone); Slott = ‘Schloss’ (castle); ‘tom’ = ‘zum’ (to the).

sentences.t: „Ach, Fru,“ säd de Mann, „de Hütt is jo god noog, wat wähl wy in’n Slott wanen.“
sentences.l: “Oh, wife,” said the man, “the cottage is indeed good enough, why would we live in a castle.”
sentences.i: “Oh, wife,” said the man, “the cottage is surely good enough; why should we live in a castle?”
sentences.n: jo = High German ‘ja/doch’; god = ‘gut’ (good); noog = ‘genug’ (enough).

sentences.t: „I wat,“ säd de Fru, „ga du man hen, de Butt kann dat jümmer doon.“
sentences.l: “Eh what,” said the wife, “just you go thither, the flounder can always do it.”
sentences.i: “Oh nonsense,” said the wife, “just go along, the flounder can always do it.”
sentences.n: ‘I wat’ = a dismissive ‘oh, what nonsense’; doon = High German ‘tun’ (to do).

sentences.t: „Ne, Fru,“ säd de Mann, „de Butt hett uns eerst de Hütt gewen, ik mag nu nich all wedder kamen, den Butt muchd et vördreten.“
sentences.l: “No, wife,” said the man, “the flounder has only just given us the cottage, I do not like to come again already, it might vex the flounder.”
sentences.i: “No, wife,” said the man, “the flounder has only just given us the cottage; I don’t like to come back so soon, it might vex the flounder.”
sentences.n: eerst = High German ‘erst’ (just now); gewen = ‘gegeben’ (given); all = ‘schon’ (already); kamen = ‘kommen’; muchd = ‘möchte’; vördreten = ‘verdrießen’ (to vex); et = ‘es.’

sentences.t: „Ga doch,“ säd de Fru, „he kann dat recht good un dait dat geern; ga du man hen.“
sentences.l: “Do go,” said the wife, “he can do it very well and does it gladly; just you go thither.”
sentences.i: “Do go,” said the wife, “he can do it very well and does it gladly; just go along.”

sentences.t: Dem Mann wöör syn Hart so swoor, un wull nich: he säd by sik sülven „dat is nich recht,“ he güng awerst doch hen.
sentences.l: The man’s heart was so heavy to him, and he would not: he said to himself, “that is not right,” but he went nevertheless thither.
sentences.i: The man’s heart grew so heavy, and he would not go; he said to himself, “This is not right” — but he went all the same.
sentences.n: Hart = High German ‘Herz’ (heart); swoor = ‘schwer’ (heavy); ‘by sik sülven’ = ‘bei sich selber’ (to himself); doch = ‘doch’ (nevertheless).

sentences.t: As he an de See köhm, wöör dat Water ganß vigelett un dunkelblau un grau un dick, un goor nich meer so gröön un geel, doch wöör’t noch still.
sentences.l: As he came to the sea, the water was all violet and dark-blue and grey and thick, and quite not so green and yellow any more, yet it was still calm.
sentences.i: When he came to the sea, the water was all violet and dark blue and grey and murky, and no longer so green and yellow, but it was still calm.
sentences.n: vigelett = High German ‘violett’ (violet); dunkelblau = ‘dunkelblau’; dick = ‘dick’ (thick, murky); still = ‘still’ (calm). The sea darkens a stage further.

sentences.t: Do güng he staan un säd
sentences.l: Then he went and stood and said
sentences.i: Then he took his stand and said:

sentences.t:
„Manntje, Manntje, Timpe Te,
Buttje, Buttje in der See,
myne Fru de Ilsebill
will nich so as ik wol will.“
sentences.l:
“Mannikin, mannikin, Timpe Te,
Flounder, flounder in the sea,
my wife the Ilsebill
will not as I would will.”
sentences.i:
“Little man, little man, Timpe Te,
Flounder, flounder in the sea,
my wife, my Ilsebill,
wills not as I would will.”
sentences.n: The same refrain again, returning as the sea grows darker (see the first note for the High German).

sentences.t: „Na, wat will se denn?“ säd de Butt.
sentences.l: “Well, what does she want then?” said the flounder.
sentences.i: “Well, what does she want, then?” said the flounder.

sentences.t: „Ach,“ säd de Mann half bedrööft, „se will in’n groot stenern Slott wanen.“
sentences.l: “Oh,” said the man, half saddened, “she wants to live in a great stone castle.”
sentences.i: “Oh,” said the man, half despondent, “she wants to live in a great stone castle.”
sentences.n: half = High German ‘halb’; bedrööft = ‘betrübt’ (saddened).

sentences.t: „Ga man hen, se stait vör der Döhr,“ säd de Butt.
sentences.l: “Just go thither, she stands before the door,” said the flounder.
sentences.i: “Just go home, she stands before the door,” said the flounder.
sentences.n: stait = High German ‘steht’ (stands); vör = ‘vor’ (before).

sentences.t: Da güng de Mann hen und dachd he wull na Huus gaan, as he awerst daar köhm, so stünn door ’n grooten stenern Pallast, un syn Fru stünn ewen up de Trepp un wull henin gaan: do nöhm se em by de Hand un säd „kumm man herein.“
sentences.l: Then the man went thither and thought he would go home; but as he came there, there stood a great stone palace, and his wife stood just on the steps and wanted to go in: then she took him by the hand and said, “just come in.”
sentences.i: Then the man went off, thinking he would go home; but when he came there, there stood a great stone palace, and his wife stood right on the steps, about to go in; then she took him by the hand and said, “Just come inside.”
sentences.n: dachd = High German ‘dachte’ (thought); daar = ‘da’ (there); Pallast = ‘Palast’ (palace); ewen = ‘eben’ (just); Trepp = ‘Treppe’ (steps); herein = ‘herein’ (in).

sentences.t: Mit des güng he mit ehr henin, un in dem Slott wöör ene groote Dehl mit marmelstenern Asters (Estrich), un dar wören so veel Bedeenters, de reten de grooten Dören up, un de Wende wören all blank un mit schöne Tapeten, un in de Zimmers luter gollne Stöhl un Dischen, un krystallen Kroonlüchters hüngen an dem Bähn, un so wöör dat all de Stuwen un Kamers mit Footdeken: un dat Aeten un de allerbeste Wyn stünn up den Dischen as wenn se breken wullen.
sentences.l: With that he went in with her, and in the castle was a great hall with a marble floor, and there were so many servants, who tore the great doors open, and the walls were all shining and hung with beautiful tapestries, and in the rooms nothing but golden chairs and tables, and crystal chandeliers hung from the ceiling, and so it was in all the parlours and chambers, with carpets: and the food and the very best wine stood upon the tables as if they would break.
sentences.i: With that he went in with her, and in the castle was a great hall with a marble floor, and there were so many servants who threw the great doors open, and the walls all shone and were hung with fine tapestries, and in the rooms nothing but golden chairs and tables, and crystal chandeliers hung from the ceiling, and so it was in all the parlours and chambers, with carpets underfoot; and the food and the very best wine stood on the tables as if they would break.
sentences.n: Dehl = High German ‘Diele’ (hall); marmelstenern = ‘marmorn’ (of marble); Asters/Estrich = the stone floor (glossed in the source); Bedeenters = ‘Bediente’ (servants); ‘reten ... up’ = ‘rissen auf’ (tore open); Dören = ‘Türen’ (doors); Wende = ‘Wände’ (walls); Zimmers = ‘Zimmer’ (rooms); luter = ‘lauter’ (nothing but); gollne = ‘goldene’ (golden); Stöhl = ‘Stühle’ (chairs); Dischen = ‘Tische(n)’ (tables); Kroonlüchters = ‘Kronleuchter’ (chandeliers); Bähn = ‘Boden/Decke’ (here the ceiling); Footdeken = ‘Fußdecken’ (carpets); Aeten = ‘Essen’ (food); Wyn = ‘Wein.’

sentences.t: Un achter dem Huse wöör ook ’n grooten Hof mit Peerd- un Kohstall, un Kutschwagens up dat allerbeste, ook was door en grooten herrlichen Goorn mit de schönnsten Blomen un fyne Aaftbömer, un en Lustholt wol ’ne halwe Myl lang, door wören Hirschen un Reh un Hasen drin un allens wat man sik jümmer wünschen mag.
sentences.l: And behind the house was also a great yard with horse- and cow-stall, and coaches of the very best; there was also a great splendid garden with the finest flowers and fine fruit-trees, and a pleasure-wood a good half-mile long, therein were stags and roe and hares and everything one could ever wish.
sentences.i: And behind the house there was also a great yard with a horse- and cow-stable, and coaches of the very best; there was too a great splendid garden with the finest flowers and fine fruit-trees, and a pleasure-wood a good half-mile long, and in it were stags and roe-deer and hares, and everything one could ever wish for.
sentences.n: Huse = High German ‘Hause’; Peerd = ‘Pferd’ (horse); Kohstall = ‘Kuhstall’ (cow-stall); Kutschwagens = ‘Kutschwagen’ (coaches); Blomen = ‘Blumen’ (flowers); fyne = ‘feine’ (fine); Aaftbömer = ‘Obstbäume’ (fruit-trees); Lustholt = ‘Lustholz/Lustwald’ (pleasure-wood); halwe = ‘halbe’ (half); Myl = ‘Meile’ (mile); Hirschen = ‘Hirsche’ (stags); Reh = ‘Reh’ (roe); Hasen = ‘Hasen’ (hares); drin = ‘drin’ (in it).

sentences.t: „Na,“ säd de Fru, „is dat nu nich schön?“
sentences.l: “Well,” said the wife, “is that not fine now?”
sentences.i: “Well,” said the wife, “isn’t that fine now?”
sentences.n: schön = High German ‘schön’ (fine, beautiful).

sentences.t: „Ach ja,“ säd de Mann, „so schall’t ook blywen, nu wähl wy ook in das schöne Slott wanen, un wähln tofreden syn.“
sentences.l: “Oh yes,” said the man, “so it shall stay too, now we will also live in the fine castle, and will be content.”
sentences.i: “Oh yes,” said the man, “so it shall stay; now we’ll live in the fine castle too and be content.”
sentences.n: wähln = High German ‘wollen’ (will); tofreden = ‘zufrieden’ (content); syn = ‘sein’ (to be).

sentences.t: „Dat wähl wy uns bedenken“ säd de Fru, „un wählen’t beslapen.“
sentences.l: “That we will think over,” said the wife, “and will sleep on it.”
sentences.i: “That we shall think over,” said the wife, “and sleep on it.”
sentences.n: ‘beslapen’ = High German ‘beschlafen’ (to sleep on, i.e. decide after a night’s sleep); wählen = ‘wollen.’

sentences.t: Mit des güngen se to Bedd.
sentences.l: With that they went to bed.

sentences.t: Den annern Morgen waakd de Fru to eerst up, dat was jüst Dag, un seeg uut jem ehr Bedd dat herrliche Land vör sik liggen.
sentences.l: The next morning the wife woke up first, it was just day, and out of their bed she saw the splendid land lying before her.
sentences.i: The next morning the wife woke first; it was just daybreak, and from their bed she saw the splendid land lying before her.
sentences.n: annern = High German ‘andern’ (next, other); ‘waakd ... up’ = ‘wachte auf’ (woke up); ‘to eerst’ = ‘zuerst’ (first); jüst = ‘gerade/just’ (just); ‘jem ehr’ = ‘ihrem’ (their); liggen = ‘liegen’ (to lie).

sentences.t: De Mann reckd sik noch, do stödd se em mit dem Ellbagen in de Syd un säd „Mann, sta up un kyk mal uut dem Fenster.
sentences.l: The man was still stretching himself, then she poked him with her elbow in the side and said, “Man, get up and look once out of the window.
sentences.i: The man was still stretching himself when she poked him in the side with her elbow and said, “Husband, get up and look out of the window.
sentences.n: ‘reckd sik’ = High German ‘reckte sich’ (stretched himself); stödd = ‘stieß’ (poked); Ellbagen = ‘Ellenbogen’ (elbow); Syd = ‘Seite’ (side); sta = ‘steh’ (get up); kyk = ‘guck/schau’ (look); uut = ‘aus.’

sentences.t: Süh, kunnen wy nich König warden äwer all düt Land?
sentences.l: Look, could we not become king over all this land?
sentences.i: See, could we not become king over all this land?
sentences.n: kunnen = High German ‘könnten’ (could); König = ‘König’ (king); warden = ‘werden’ (to become); äwer = ‘über’ (over); düt = ‘dies’ (this).

sentences.t: Ga hen tom Butt, wy wählt König syn.“
sentences.l: Go thither to the flounder, we will be king.”
sentences.i: Go to the flounder, we will be king.”
sentences.n: wählt = High German ‘wollen’ (will).

sentences.t: „Ach, Fru,“ säd de Mann, „wat wähl wy König syn! ik mag nich König syn.“
sentences.l: “Oh, wife,” said the man, “what would we be king for! I do not want to be king.”
sentences.i: “Oh, wife,” said the man, “why should we be king! I don’t want to be king.”

sentences.t: „Na,“ säd de Fru, „wult du nich König syn, so will ik König syn.
sentences.l: “Well,” said the wife, “if you will not be king, then I will be king.
sentences.n: wult = High German ‘willst’ (want); will = ‘will.’

sentences.t: Ga hen tom Butt, ik will König syn.“
sentences.l: Go thither to the flounder, I will be king.”

sentences.t: „Ach, Fru,“ säd de Mann, „wat wullst du König syn? dat mag ik em nich seggen.“
sentences.l: “Oh, wife,” said the man, “what would you be king for? I do not like to tell him that.”
sentences.i: “Oh, wife,” said the man, “why would you be king? I don’t like to tell him that.”
sentences.n: wullst = High German ‘willst’ (want); seggen = ‘sagen’ (to say).

sentences.t: „Worüm nich?“ säd de Fru, „ga stracks hen, ik mutt König syn.“
sentences.l: “Why not?” said the wife, “go straightway thither, I must be king.”
sentences.i: “Why not?” said the wife, “go at once, I must be king.”
sentences.n: worüm = High German ‘warum’ (why); stracks = ‘stracks/sofort’ (straightway); mutt = ‘muss’ (must).

sentences.t: Do güng de Mann hen un wöör ganß bedröft dat syne Fru König warden wull.
sentences.l: Then the man went thither and was quite saddened that his wife wanted to become king.
sentences.i: Then the man went off, quite grieved that his wife wanted to become king.
sentences.n: bedröft = High German ‘betrübt’ (saddened).

sentences.t: „Dat is nich recht un is nich recht,“ dachd de Mann.
sentences.l: “That is not right and is not right,” thought the man.
sentences.i: “It is not right, it is not right,” thought the man.

sentences.t: He wull nich hen gaan, güng awerst doch hen.
sentences.l: He would not go thither, but went nevertheless thither.
sentences.i: He would not go, but went all the same.

sentences.t: Un as he an de See köhm, do wöör de See ganß swartgrau, un dat Water geerd so von ünnen up un stünk ook ganß fuul.
sentences.l: And as he came to the sea, the sea was all black-grey, and the water heaved so up from below and stank also quite foul.
sentences.i: And when he came to the sea, the sea was all blackish-grey, and the water heaved up from below and stank quite foul.
sentences.n: swartgrau = High German ‘schwarzgrau’ (blackish-grey); geerd = ‘gärte’ (heaved, fermented); ‘von ünnen up’ = ‘von unten auf’ (up from below); ünnen = ‘unten’; stünk = ‘stank’; fuul = ‘faul’ (foul). The sea worsens again.

sentences.t: Do güng he staan un säd
sentences.l: Then he went and stood and said
sentences.i: Then he took his stand and said:

sentences.t:
„Manntje, Manntje, Timpe Te,
Buttje, Buttje in der See,
sentences.l:
“Mannikin, mannikin, Timpe Te,
Flounder, flounder in the sea,
sentences.i:
“Little man, little man, Timpe Te,
Flounder, flounder in the sea,
sentences.n: Here the source prints the refrain in two halves; this is the first half (see the first refrain note for the full High German).

sentences.t:
myne Fru de Ilsebill
will nich so as ik wol will.“
sentences.l:
my wife the Ilsebill
will not as I would will.”
sentences.i:
my wife, my Ilsebill,
wills not as I would will.”

sentences.t: „Na, wat will se denn?“ säd de Butt.
sentences.l: “Well, what does she want then?” said the flounder.
sentences.i: “Well, what does she want, then?” said the flounder.

sentences.t: „Ach,“ säd de Mann, „se will König warden.“
sentences.l: “Oh,” said the man, “she wants to become king.”

sentences.t: „Ga man hen, se is’t all,“ säd de Butt.
sentences.l: “Just go thither, she is it already,” said the flounder.
sentences.i: “Just go home, she is it already,” said the flounder.
sentences.n: ‘se is’t all’ = ‘sie ist es schon’ (she is it already); is = High German ‘ist.’

sentences.t: Do güng de Mann hen, un as he na dem Pallast köhm, so wöör dat Slott veel grötter worren, mit enem grooten Toorn un herrlyken Zyraat doran: un de Schildwacht stünn vor de Döhr, un dar wören so väle Soldaten un Pauken un Trumpeten.
sentences.l: Then the man went thither, and as he came to the palace, the castle had become much bigger, with a great tower and splendid ornament thereon: and the sentry stood before the door, and there were so many soldiers and kettledrums and trumpets.
sentences.i: Then the man went off, and when he came to the palace, the castle had grown much bigger, with a great tower and splendid ornament on it; and the sentry stood before the door, and there were so many soldiers and kettledrums and trumpets.
sentences.n: worren = High German ‘geworden’ (become); Toorn = ‘Turm’ (tower); herrlyken = ‘herrlichen’ (splendid); Zyraat = ‘Zierat’ (ornament); doran = ‘daran’ (thereon); Schildwacht = ‘Schildwache’ (sentry); väle = ‘viele’ (many); Pauken = ‘Pauken’ (kettledrums); Trumpeten = ‘Trompeten’ (trumpets).

sentences.t: Un as he in dat Huus köhm, so wöör allens von purem Marmelsteen mit Gold, un sammtne Deken un groote gollne Quasten.
sentences.l: And as he came into the house, everything was of pure marble with gold, and velvet hangings and great golden tassels.
sentences.i: And when he came into the house, everything was of pure marble and gold, with velvet hangings and great golden tassels.
sentences.n: purem = High German ‘purem’ (pure); Marmelsteen = ‘Marmorstein’ (marble); sammtne = ‘samtene’ (velvet); Deken = ‘Decken’ (hangings, covers); Quasten = ‘Quasten’ (tassels).

sentences.t: Do güngen de Dören von dem Saal up, door de ganße Hofstaat wöör, un syne Fru seet up enem hogen Troon von Gold un Demant, un hadd ene groote gollne Kroon up un den Zepter in der Hand von purem Gold un Edelsteen, un up beyden Syden by ehr stünnen ses Jumpfern in ene Reeg, jümmer ene enen Kops lüttjer as de annere.
sentences.l: Then the doors of the hall went open, where the whole court was, and his wife sat on a high throne of gold and diamond, and had a great golden crown on and the sceptre in her hand of pure gold and precious stone, and on both sides by her stood six maidens in a row, each one a head littler than the other.
sentences.i: Then the doors of the hall opened, where the whole court was assembled, and his wife sat on a high throne of gold and diamond, wearing a great golden crown and holding the sceptre of pure gold and jewels in her hand; and on either side of her stood six maidens in a row, each one a head shorter than the last.
sentences.n: Saal = High German ‘Saal’ (hall); Hofstaat = ‘Hofstaat’ (court, household); hogen = ‘hohen’ (high); Troon = ‘Thron’ (throne); Demant = ‘Demant/Diamant’ (diamond); Kroon = ‘Krone’ (crown); Zepter = ‘Zepter’ (sceptre); Edelsteen = ‘Edelstein’ (jewel); ‘beyden Syden’ = ‘beiden Seiten’ (both sides); ehr = ‘ihr’ (her); ses = ‘sechs’ (six); Jumpfern = ‘Jungfern’ (maidens); Reeg = ‘Reihe’ (row); Kops = ‘Kopf’ (head); lüttjer = ‘kleiner’ (littler); annere = ‘andere’ (other).

sentences.t: Do güng he staan und säd „ach Fru, büst du nu König?“
sentences.l: Then he went and stood and said, “oh wife, are you now king?”
sentences.i: Then he took his stand and said, “Oh wife, are you king now?”
sentences.n: büst = High German ‘bist’ (are); nu = ‘nun’ (now).

sentences.t: „Ja,“ säd de Fru, „nu bün ik König.“
sentences.l: “Yes,” said the wife, “now I am king.”

sentences.t: Do stünn he un seeg se an, un as he se do een Flach (eine Zeit lang) so ansehn hadd, säd he „ach, Fru, wat lett dat schöön, wenn du König büst! nu wähl wy ook niks meer wünschen.“
sentences.l: Then he stood and looked at her, and when he had so looked at her for a while, he said, “oh, wife, how fine that looks, when you are king! now we will wish nothing more.”
sentences.i: Then he stood and gazed at her, and when he had gazed at her a while, he said, “Oh, wife, how fine it looks when you are king! Now we’ll wish for nothing more.”
sentences.n: ‘een Flach’ = a while (the Grimms gloss it in the source as ‘eine Zeit lang’); Flach = a stretch of time. ‘lett dat schöön’ = ‘das lässt schön’ (that looks fine, becoming); lett = High German ‘lässt’; niks = ‘nichts.’

sentences.t: „Ne, Mann,“ säd de Fru, un wöör ganß unruhig, „my waart de Tyd und Wyl al lang, ik kann dat nich meer uuthollen.
sentences.l: “No, man,” said the wife, and was quite restless, “to me the time and while grows already long, I can bear it no more.
sentences.i: “No, husband,” said the wife, and grew quite restless, “time hangs heavy on me now, I can bear it no longer.
sentences.n: unruhig = High German ‘unruhig’ (restless); ‘my waart de Tyd un Wyl al lang’ = ‘mir wird die Zeit und Weile schon lang’ (time grows long for me, I grow impatient); waart = ‘wird’ (grows); Tyd = ‘Zeit’ (time); Wyl = ‘Weile’ (while); al = ‘schon’ (already); uuthollen = ‘aushalten’ (to bear).

sentences.t: Ga hen tom Butt, König bün ik, nu mutt ik ook Kaiser warden.“
sentences.l: Go thither to the flounder, king am I, now I must also become emperor.”
sentences.i: Go to the flounder; king I am, now I must become emperor too.”
sentences.n: Kaiser = High German ‘Kaiser’ (emperor). The wishes now climb past king — emperor, then pope, then to be like God.

sentences.t: „Ach, Fru,“ säd de Mann, „wat wullst du Kaiser warden?“
sentences.l: “Oh, wife,” said the man, “what would you become emperor for?”
sentences.i: “Oh, wife,” said the man, “why would you become emperor?”

sentences.t: „Mann,“ säd se, „ga tom Butt, ik will Kaiser syn.“
sentences.l: “Man,” said she, “go to the flounder, I will be emperor.”
sentences.i: “Husband,” said she, “go to the flounder, I will be emperor.”

sentences.t: „Ach, Fru,“ säd de Mann, „Kaiser kann he nich maken, ik mag dem Butt dat nich seggen; Kaiser is man eenmal im Reich: Kaiser kann de Butt jo nich maken, dat kann un kann he nich.“
sentences.l: “Oh, wife,” said the man, “emperor he cannot make, I do not like to tell the flounder that; emperor there is only one in the realm: the flounder surely cannot make emperor, that he cannot and cannot do.”
sentences.i: “Oh, wife,” said the man, “emperor he cannot make; I don’t like to tell the flounder that. There is only one emperor in the realm; the flounder simply cannot make an emperor, that he cannot and cannot do.”
sentences.n: maken = High German ‘machen’ (to make); ‘man eenmal’ = ‘nur einmal’ (only one); eenmal = ‘einmal’; im Reich = ‘im Reich’ (in the realm).

sentences.t: „Wat,“ säd de Fru, „ik bünn König un du bist man myn Mann, wullt du glyk hengaan? glyk ga hen, kann he König maken, kann he ook Kaiser maken, ik will un will Kaiser syn; glyk ga hen.“
sentences.l: “What,” said the wife, “I am king and you are only my husband, will you go at once? go at once, if he can make king, he can also make emperor, I will and will be emperor; go at once.”
sentences.i: “What,” said the wife, “I am king, and you are only my husband — will you go at once? Go this instant; if he can make a king, he can make an emperor too; I will and will be emperor; go at once.”
sentences.n: bünn = High German ‘bin’ (am, a doubled-n variant of bün); bist = ‘bist’ (are).

sentences.t: Do mussd he hengaan.
sentences.l: Then he had to go thither.
sentences.i: Then he had to go.
sentences.n: mussd = High German ‘musste’ (had to).

sentences.t: Do de Mann awer hengüng, wöör em ganß bang, un as he so güng, dachd he by sik „düt gait un gait nich good: Kaiser is to uutvörschaamt, de Butt wart am Ende möd.“
sentences.l: But when the man went off, he grew quite afraid, and as he went, he thought to himself, “this goes and goes not well: emperor is too shameless, the flounder will grow weary at the end.”
sentences.i: But as the man went off, he grew quite afraid, and as he walked he thought to himself, “This is going nowhere good; emperor is too shameless — in the end the flounder will grow weary.”
sentences.n: hengüng = High German ‘hinging’ (went off); bang = ‘bange’ (afraid); gait = ‘geht’ (goes); uutvörschaamt = ‘unverschämt’ (shameless, brazen); wart = ‘wird’ (will); ‘am Ende’ = ‘am Ende’ (in the end); möd = ‘müde’ (weary).

sentences.t: Mit des köhm he an de See, do wöör de See noch ganß swart un dick un füng al so von ünnen up to geeren, dat et so Blasen smeet, un et güng so ein Keekwind äwer hen, dat et sik so köhrd; un de Mann wurr groen (grauen).
sentences.l: With that he came to the sea, the sea was still all black and thick and already began so to heave up from below, that it threw up bubbles so, and such a squall-wind went over across, that it churned itself so; and the man grew afraid.
sentences.i: With that he came to the sea, and the sea was still all black and murky, and already began to heave up from below so that it threw up bubbles, and such a squall swept across it that it churned; and the man grew afraid.
sentences.n: ‘füng ... to geeren’ = High German ‘fing an zu gären’ (began to heave, ferment); Blasen = ‘Blasen’ (bubbles); smeet = ‘schmiss/warf’ (threw); Keekwind = a gusting squall-wind; ‘äwer hen’ = ‘darüber hin’; köhrd = ‘kehrte’ (churned, turned); wurr = ‘wurde’ (grew); groen = ‘grauen’ (to grow afraid, shudder — glossed in the source).

sentences.t: Do güng he staan un säd
sentences.l: Then he went and stood and said
sentences.i: Then he took his stand and said:

sentences.t:
„Manntje, Manntje, Timpe Te,
Buttje, Buttje in der See,
myne Fru de Ilsebill
will nich so as ik wol will.“
sentences.l:
“Mannikin, mannikin, Timpe Te,
Flounder, flounder in the sea,
my wife the Ilsebill
will not as I would will.”
sentences.i:
“Little man, little man, Timpe Te,
Flounder, flounder in the sea,
my wife, my Ilsebill,
wills not as I would will.”
sentences.n: The refrain once more, as the sea turns black and begins to heave.

sentences.t: „Na, wat will se denn?“ säd de Butt.
sentences.l: “Well, what does she want then?” said the flounder.
sentences.i: “Well, what does she want, then?” said the flounder.

sentences.t: „Ach, Butt,“ säd he, „myn Fru will Kaiser warden.“
sentences.l: “Oh, flounder,” said he, “my wife wants to become emperor.”

sentences.t: „Ga man hen,“ säd de Butt, „se is’t all.“
sentences.l: “Just go thither,” said the flounder, “she is it already.”
sentences.i: “Just go home,” said the flounder, “she is it already.”

sentences.t: Do güng de Mann hen, un as he door köhm, so wöör dat ganße Slott von poleertem Marmelsteen mit albasternen Figuren und gollnen Zyraten.
sentences.l: Then the man went thither, and as he came there, the whole castle was of polished marble with alabaster figures and golden ornaments.
sentences.i: Then the man went off, and when he came there, the whole castle was of polished marble with alabaster figures and golden ornaments.
sentences.n: poleertem = High German ‘poliertem’ (polished); albasternen = ‘alabasternen’ (alabaster); Figuren = ‘Figuren’ (figures); gollnen = ‘goldenen’ (golden); Zyraten = ‘Zieraten’ (ornaments).

sentences.t: Vör de Döhr marscheerden de Soldaten, un se blösen Trumpeten und slögen Pauken un Trummeln: awerst in dem Huse da güngen de Baronen un Grawen un Herzogen man so as Bedeenters herüm: do maakden se em de Dören up, de von luter Gold wören.
sentences.l: Before the door the soldiers marched, and they blew trumpets and beat kettledrums and drums: but in the house there the barons and counts and dukes went about just like servants: then they made the doors open for him, which were of nothing but gold.
sentences.i: Before the door the soldiers marched, and they blew trumpets and beat kettledrums and drums; but inside the house the barons and counts and dukes went about just like servants; then they opened the doors for him, which were of nothing but gold.
sentences.n: marscheerden = High German ‘marschierten’ (marched); blösen = ‘bliesen’ (blew); slögen = ‘schlugen’ (beat); Trummeln = ‘Trommeln’ (drums); Baronen = ‘Barone’ (barons); Grawen = ‘Grafen’ (counts); Herzogen = ‘Herzöge’ (dukes); herüm = ‘herum’ (about); maakden = ‘machten’ (made).

sentences.t: Und as he herinköhm, door seet syne Fru up enem Troon, de wöör von een Stück Gold, un wör wol twe Myl hoog: un hadd ene groote gollne Kroon up, de wöör dre Elen hoog un mit Briljanten un Karfunkelsteen besett’t: in de ene Hand hadde se den Zepter un in de annere Hand den Reichsappel, un up beyden Syden by eer door stünnen de Trabanten so in twe Regen, jümmer een lüttjer as de annere, von dem allergröttesten Rysen, de wöör twe Myl hoog, bet to dem allerlüttjesten Dwaark, de wöör man so groot as min lüttje Finger.
sentences.l: And as he came in, there sat his wife on a throne, which was of one piece of gold, and was surely two miles high: and had a great golden crown on, which was three ells high and set with brilliants and carbuncle: in the one hand she had the sceptre and in the other hand the imperial orb, and on both sides by her there stood the guards so in two rows, each one littler than the other, from the very biggest giant, who was two miles high, to the very littlest dwarf, who was only as big as my little finger.
sentences.i: And when he came in, there sat his wife on a throne that was of one piece of gold and was fully two miles high; and she wore a great golden crown, three ells high and set with brilliants and carbuncles; in one hand she held the sceptre and in the other the imperial orb, and on either side of her the guards stood in two rows, each one smaller than the last, from the very biggest giant, who was two miles high, down to the very littlest dwarf, who was only as big as my little finger.
sentences.n: wör = High German ‘wurde/war’; twe = ‘zwei’ (two); hoog = ‘hoch’ (high); dre = ‘drei’ (three); Elen = ‘Ellen’ (ells); Briljanten = ‘Brillanten’ (brilliants); Karfunkelsteen = ‘Karfunkelstein’ (carbuncle); besett = ‘besetzt’ (set, studded); Reichsappel = ‘Reichsapfel’ (imperial orb); eer = ‘ihr’ (her); Trabanten = ‘Trabanten’ (guards); Regen = ‘Reihen’ (rows); allergröttesten = ‘allergrößten’ (very biggest); Rysen = ‘Riesen’ (giant); bet = ‘bis’ (to); allerlüttjesten = ‘allerkleinsten’ (very littlest); Dwaark = ‘Zwerg’ (dwarf); min = ‘mein’ (my); Finger = ‘Finger.’

sentences.t: Un vör ehr stünnen so vele Fürsten un Herzogen.
sentences.l: And before her stood so many princes and dukes.
sentences.n: vele = High German ‘viele’ (many); Fürsten = ‘Fürsten’ (princes).

sentences.t: Door güng de Mann tüschen staan un säd „Fru, büst du nu Kaiser?“
sentences.l: There the man went and stood among them and said, “wife, are you now emperor?”
sentences.i: There the man took his stand among them and said, “Wife, are you emperor now?”
sentences.n: tüschen = High German ‘zwischen’ (between, among).

sentences.t: „Ja,“ säd se, „ik bün Kaiser.“
sentences.l: “Yes,” said she, “I am emperor.”

sentences.t: Do güng he staan un beseeg se sik so recht, un as he se so’n Flach ansehn hadd, so säd he „ach, Fru, watt lett dat schöön, wenn du Kaiser büst.“
sentences.l: Then he stood and looked her over rightly, and when he had looked at her such a while, he said, “oh, wife, how fine that looks, when you are emperor.”
sentences.i: Then he stood and looked her well over, and when he had gazed at her a while, he said, “Oh, wife, how fine it looks when you are emperor.”
sentences.n: beseeg = High German ‘besah’ (looked over); ‘so’n Flach’ = ‘so eine Weile’ (such a while); watt = ‘was.’

sentences.t: „Mann,“ säd se, „wat staist du door? ik bün nu Kaiser, nu will ik awerst ook Paabst warden, ga hen tom Butt.“
sentences.l: “Man,” said she, “what do you stand there? I am now emperor, now I will however also become pope, go thither to the flounder.”
sentences.i: “Husband,” said she, “why do you stand there? I am emperor now, but now I will become pope too; go to the flounder.”
sentences.n: staist = High German ‘stehst’ (stand); Paabst = ‘Papst’ (pope).

sentences.t: „Ach, Fru,“ säd de Mann, „wat wulst du man nich? Paabst kannst du nich warden, Paabst is man eenmaal in der Kristenhait, dat kann he doch nich maken.“
sentences.l: “Oh, wife,” said the man, “what would you not want? pope you cannot become, there is only one pope in Christendom, that he surely cannot make.”
sentences.i: “Oh, wife,” said the man, “what will you not want next? Pope you cannot become; there is only one pope in Christendom, that the flounder surely cannot make.”
sentences.n: wulst = High German ‘willst’ (want); eenmaal = ‘einmal’; Kristenhait = ‘Christenheit’ (Christendom).

sentences.t: „Mann,“ säd se, „ik will Paabst warden, ga glyk hen, ik mutt hüüt noch Paabst warden.“
sentences.l: “Man,” said she, “I will become pope, go at once thither, I must become pope this very day.”
sentences.i: “Husband,” said she, “I will become pope; go at once, I must become pope this very day.”

sentences.t: „Ne, Fru,“ säd de Mann, „dat mag ik em nich seggen, dat gait nich good, dat is to groff, tom Paabst kann de Butt nich maken.“
sentences.l: “No, wife,” said the man, “I do not like to tell him that, that goes not well, that is too gross, the flounder cannot make pope.”
sentences.i: “No, wife,” said the man, “I don’t like to tell him that, it will go ill, it is too gross; the flounder cannot make you pope.”
sentences.n: groff = High German ‘grob’ (gross, coarse).

sentences.t: „Mann, wat Snack!“ säd de Fru, „kann he Kaiser maken, kann he ook Paabst maken.
sentences.l: “Man, what chatter!” said the wife, “if he can make emperor, he can also make pope.
sentences.i: “Husband, what nonsense!” said the wife, “if he can make an emperor, he can make a pope too.
sentences.n: Snack = High German ‘Schnack/Geschwätz’ (chatter, nonsense).

sentences.t: Ga foorts hen, ik bünn Kaiser un du büst man myn Mann, wullt du wol hengaan?“
sentences.l: Go straightway thither, I am emperor and you are only my husband, will you indeed go?”
sentences.i: Go at once; I am emperor and you are only my husband — will you go?”
sentences.n: foorts = High German ‘fort/sofort’ (straightway).

sentences.t: Do wurr he bang un güng hen, em wöör awerst ganß flau, un zitterd un beewd, un de Knee un de Waden slakkerden em.
sentences.l: Then he grew afraid and went thither, but he felt quite faint, and trembled and quaked, and the knees and the calves shook on him.
sentences.i: Then he grew afraid and went off, but he felt quite faint, and trembled and shook, and his knees and calves quivered under him.
sentences.n: flau = High German ‘flau’ (faint); zitterd = ‘zitterte’ (trembled); beewd = ‘bebte’ (quaked); Knee = ‘Knie’ (knees); Waden = ‘Waden’ (calves); slakkerden = ‘schlackerten’ (shook).

sentences.t: Un dar streek so’n Wind äwer dat Land, un de Wolken flögen, as dat düster wurr gegen Awend: de Bläder waiden von den Bömern, un dat Water güng un bruusd as kaakd dat, un platschd an dat Aever, un von feern seeg he de Schepen, de schöten in der Noot, un danßden un sprüngen up den Bülgen.
sentences.l: And there swept such a wind over the land, and the clouds flew, as it grew dark toward evening: the leaves blew from the trees, and the water surged and roared as if it boiled, and splashed against the shore, and from afar he saw the ships, which fired in distress, and danced and sprang upon the billows.
sentences.i: And such a wind swept over the land, and the clouds flew as it grew dark toward evening; the leaves blew from the trees, and the water surged and roared as though it boiled, and splashed against the shore, and from afar he saw the ships firing signals of distress, dancing and leaping upon the billows.
sentences.n: streek = High German ‘strich’ (swept); flögen = ‘flogen’ (flew); düster = ‘düster’ (dark); ‘gegen Awend’ = ‘gegen Abend’ (toward evening); Awend = ‘Abend’; Bläder = ‘Blätter’ (leaves); waiden = ‘wehten’ (blew); Bömern = ‘Bäumen’ (trees); bruusd = ‘brauste’ (roared); kaakd = ‘kochte’ (boiled); platschd = ‘platschte’ (splashed); Aever = ‘Ufer’ (shore); feern = ‘fern’ (afar); Schepen = ‘Schiffe’ (ships); schöten = ‘schossen’ (fired); Noot = ‘Not’ (distress); danßden = ‘tanzten’ (danced); sprüngen = ‘sprangen’ (sprang); Bülgen = ‘Wogen’ (billows).

sentences.t: Doch wöör de Himmel noch so’n bitten blau in de Midd, awerst an den Syden door toog dat so recht rood up as en swohr Gewitter.
sentences.l: Yet the sky was still a little bit blue in the middle, but at the sides it drew up so right red like a heavy thunderstorm.
sentences.i: Yet the sky was still a little blue in the middle, but at the edges it was drawing up as red as a heavy thunderstorm.
sentences.n: ‘so’n bitten’ = High German ‘so ein bisschen’ (a little bit); Midd = ‘Mitte’ (middle); ‘toog ... up’ = ‘zog auf’ (drew up); toog = ‘zog’; rood = ‘rot’ (red); swohr = ‘schwer’ (heavy); Gewitter = ‘Gewitter’ (thunderstorm). The reddening sky is the tale’s clearest warning that the wishing has gone too far.

sentences.t: Do güng he recht vörzufft (verzagt) staan in de Angst un säd
sentences.l: Then, right disheartened, he went and stood in his fear and said
sentences.i: Then, right disheartened, he took his stand in his fear and said:
sentences.n: vörzufft = High German ‘verzagt’ (disheartened — glossed in the source); Angst = ‘Angst’ (fear).

sentences.t:
„Manntje, Manntje, Timpe Te,
Buttje, Buttje in der See,
myne Fru de Ilsebill,
will nich so as ik wol will.“
sentences.l:
“Mannikin, mannikin, Timpe Te,
Flounder, flounder in the sea,
my wife the Ilsebill
will not as I would will.”
sentences.i:
“Little man, little man, Timpe Te,
Flounder, flounder in the sea,
my wife, my Ilsebill,
wills not as I would will.”
sentences.n: The refrain again, now cried in real fear.

sentences.t: „Na, wat will se denn?“ säd de Butt.
sentences.l: “Well, what does she want then?” said the flounder.
sentences.i: “Well, what does she want, then?” said the flounder.

sentences.t: „Ach,“ säd de Mann, „se will Paabst warden.“
sentences.l: “Oh,” said the man, “she wants to become pope.”

sentences.t: „Ga man hen, se is’t all“ säd de Butt.
sentences.l: “Just go thither, she is it already,” said the flounder.
sentences.i: “Just go home, she is it already,” said the flounder.

sentences.t: Do güng he hen, un as he door köhm, so wöör dar as en groote Kirch mit luter Pallastens ümgewen.
sentences.l: Then he went thither, and as he came there, it was there like a great church surrounded with nothing but palaces.
sentences.i: Then he went off, and when he came there, it was like a great church, surrounded with nothing but palaces.
sentences.n: Kirch = High German ‘Kirche’ (church); Pallastens = ‘Palästen’ (palaces); ümgewen = ‘umgeben’ (surrounded).

sentences.t: Door drängd he sik dorch dat Volk: inwendig was awer allens mit dausend un dausend Lichtern erleuchtet, un syne Fru wöör in luter Gold gekledet, un seet noch up enem veel högeren Troon, un hadde dre groote gollne Kronen up, un üm ehr dar so veel von geistlykem Staat, un up beyden Syden by ehr door stünnen twe Regen Lichter, dat gröttste so dick un groot as de allergröttste Toorn, bet to dem allerkleensten Käkenlicht; un alle de Kaisers un de Königen de legen vör ehr up de Kne un küßden ehr den Tüffel.
sentences.l: There he pressed himself through the folk: inside, however, everything was lit with thousand and thousand lights, and his wife was clad in nothing but gold, and sat on a much higher throne still, and had three great golden crowns on, and around her so much of churchly state, and on both sides by her stood two rows of lights, the biggest as thick and big as the very biggest tower, down to the very smallest kitchen-candle; and all the emperors and the kings, they lay before her on the knee and kissed her the slipper.
sentences.i: There he pressed his way through the throng; inside, everything was lit with thousands upon thousands of lights, and his wife was clad all in gold, and sat on a throne higher still, and wore three great golden crowns; and about her was so much churchly pomp, and on either side of her stood two rows of candles, the biggest as thick and tall as the very biggest tower, down to the very smallest kitchen-candle; and all the emperors and kings lay before her on their knees and kissed her slipper.
sentences.n: drängd = High German ‘drängte’ (pressed); dorch = ‘durch’ (through); Volk = ‘Volk’ (folk); inwendig = ‘inwendig/innen’ (inside); dausend = ‘tausend’ (thousand); Lichtern = ‘Lichtern’ (lights); erleuchtet = ‘erleuchtet’ (lit); gekledet = ‘gekleidet’ (clad); högeren = ‘höheren’ (higher); Kronen = ‘Kronen’ (crowns); üm = ‘um’ (around); geistlykem = ‘geistlichem’ (spiritual, churchly); Staat = ‘Staat/Pracht’ (pomp); Käkenlicht = ‘Küchenlicht’ (kitchen-candle); allerkleensten = ‘allerkleinsten’ (very smallest); Kaisers = ‘Kaiser’ (emperors); Königen = ‘Könige’ (kings); legen = ‘lagen’ (lay); Kne = ‘Knie’ (knee); küßden = ‘küssten’ (kissed); Tüffel = ‘Pantoffel’ (slipper).

sentences.t: „Fru,“ säd de Mann un seeg se so recht an, „büst du nu Paabst?“
sentences.l: “Wife,” said the man and looked at her rightly, “are you now pope?”
sentences.i: “Wife,” said the man, looking her full in the face, “are you pope now?”

sentences.t: „Ja,“ säd se, „ik bün Paabst.“
sentences.l: “Yes,” said she, “I am pope.”

sentences.t: Do güng he staan un seeg se recht an, un dat wöör as wenn he in de hell Sunn seeg.
sentences.l: Then he stood and looked at her rightly, and it was as when he looked into the bright sun.
sentences.i: Then he stood and gazed at her, and it was as if he looked into the bright sun.
sentences.n: hell = High German ‘helle’ (bright); Sunn = ‘Sonne’ (sun).

sentences.t: As he se do en Flach ansehn hadd, so segt he „ach, Fru, wat lett dat schöön, wenn du Paabst büst!“
sentences.l: When he had looked at her such a while, he says, “oh, wife, how fine that looks, when you are pope!”
sentences.i: When he had gazed at her a while, he said, “Oh, wife, how fine it looks when you are pope!”
sentences.n: segt = High German ‘sagt’ (says) — a present form slipping into the past narration.

sentences.t: Se seet awerst ganß styf as en Boom, un rüppeld un röhrd sik nich.
sentences.l: But she sat quite stiff like a tree, and neither stirred nor moved herself.
sentences.i: But she sat quite stiff as a tree, and neither stirred nor moved.
sentences.n: styf = High German ‘steif’ (stiff); Boom = ‘Baum’ (tree); rüppeld = ‘rührte sich/ruppelte’ (stirred); röhrd = ‘rührte’ (moved).

sentences.t: Do säd he „Fru, nu sy tofreden, nu du Paabst büst, nu kannst du doch niks meer warden.“
sentences.l: Then he said, “wife, now be content, now that you are pope, now you can surely become nothing more.”
sentences.i: Then he said, “Wife, now be content; now that you are pope, you can become nothing more.”
sentences.n: sy = High German ‘sei’ (be).

sentences.t: „Dat will ik my bedenken“ säd de Fru.
sentences.l: “That I will think over,” said the wife.
sentences.i: “That I shall think over,” said the wife.

sentences.t: Mit des güngen se beyde to Bedd, awerst se wöör nich tofreden, un de Girighait leet se nich slapen, se dachd jümmer wat se noch warden wull.
sentences.l: With that they both went to bed, but she was not content, and the greed let her not sleep, she thought ever what she still wanted to become.
sentences.i: With that they both went to bed, but she was not content, and greed would not let her sleep; she thought ever of what she might yet become.
sentences.n: beyde = High German ‘beide’ (both); Girighait = ‘Gierigkeit/Gier’ (greed); slapen = ‘schlafen’ (to sleep).

sentences.t: De Mann sleep recht good un fast, he hadd den Dag veel lopen, de Fru awerst kunn goor nich inslapen, un smeet sik von een Syd to der annern de ganße Nacht un dachd man jümmer wat se noch wol warden kunn, un kunn sik doch up niks meer besinnen.
sentences.l: The man slept right well and fast, he had walked much that day, but the wife could not fall asleep at all, and threw herself from one side to the other the whole night and thought ever what she still might become, and yet could bethink herself of nothing more.
sentences.i: The man slept well and soundly, for he had walked much that day; but the wife could not fall asleep at all, and tossed from one side to the other the whole night, thinking ever of what she might yet become, and could think of nothing more.
sentences.n: sleep = High German ‘schlief’ (slept); fast = ‘fest’ (soundly); lopen = ‘gelaufen’ (walked); inslapen = ‘einschlafen’ (to fall asleep); ‘smeet sik’ = ‘schmiss/warf sich’ (tossed); besinnen = ‘besinnen’ (to bethink).

sentences.t: Mit des wull de Sünn upgaan, un as se dat Morgenrood seeg, richt’d se sik äwer End im Bedd un seeg door henin, un as se uut dem Fenster de Sünn so herup kamen seeg, „ha,“ dachd se, „kunn ik nich ook de Sünn un de Maan upgaan laten?“
sentences.l: With that the sun would rise, and as she saw the dawn-red, she raised herself upright in the bed and looked out into it, and as she saw out of the window the sun so come up, “ha,” she thought, “could I not also let the sun and the moon rise?”
sentences.i: With that the sun was about to rise, and when she saw the red of dawn, she raised herself up in the bed and looked out, and when she saw through the window the sun come up, “Ha,” she thought, “could I not make the sun and the moon rise too?”
sentences.n: Sünn = High German ‘Sonne’ (sun); upgaan = ‘aufgehen’ (to rise); Morgenrood = ‘Morgenrot’ (red of dawn); ‘richt’d sik äwer End’ = ‘richtete sich über Ende auf’ (raised herself upright); herup = ‘herauf’ (up); Maan = ‘Mond’ (moon). The final, blasphemous wish begins to form: to command the heavens like God.

sentences.t: „Mann,“ säd se un stödd em mit dem Ellbagen in de Ribben, „waak up, ga hen tom Butt, ik will warden as de lewe Gott.“
sentences.l: “Man,” said she and poked him with the elbow in the ribs, “wake up, go thither to the flounder, I will become like the dear God.”
sentences.i: “Husband,” said she, poking him in the ribs with her elbow, “wake up, go to the flounder, I will become like the dear Lord.”
sentences.n: Ribben = High German ‘Rippen’ (ribs); ‘waak up’ = ‘wach auf’ (wake up); ‘de lewe Gott’ = ‘der liebe Gott’ (the dear Lord); lewe = ‘liebe’ (dear); Gott = ‘Gott’ (God). This is the last and worst wish — to be like God.

sentences.t: De Mann was noch meist in’n Slaap, awerst he vörschrock sik so, dat he uut dem Bedd füll.
sentences.l: The man was still mostly in sleep, but he took such a fright that he fell out of the bed.
sentences.i: The man was still half asleep, but he took such a fright that he fell out of bed.
sentences.n: meist = High German ‘meist/fast’ (mostly, almost); Slaap = ‘Schlaf’ (sleep); ‘vörschrock sik’ = ‘erschrak’ (took fright); füll = ‘fiel’ (fell).

sentences.t: He meend he hadd sik vörhöörd un reef sik de Ogen uut un säd „ach, Fru, wat säd’st du?“
sentences.l: He thought he had misheard himself and rubbed his eyes and said, “oh, wife, what did you say?”
sentences.i: He thought he had misheard, and rubbed his eyes, and said, “Oh, wife, what did you say?”
sentences.n: meend = High German ‘meinte’ (thought); vörhöörd = ‘verhört’ (misheard); reef = ‘rieb’ (rubbed); Ogen = ‘Augen’ (eyes); ‘säd’st’ = ‘sagtest’ (did say).

sentences.t: „Mann,“ säd se, „wenn ik nich de Sünn un de Maan kan upgaan laten, un mutt dat so ansehn, dat de Sünn un de Maan upgaan, ik kann dat nich uuthollen, un hebb kene geruhige Stünd meer, dat ik se nich sülwst kann upgaan laten.“
sentences.l: “Man,” said she, “if I cannot let the sun and the moon rise, and must look on while the sun and the moon rise, I cannot bear it, and have not one quiet hour more, since I cannot myself let them rise.”
sentences.i: “Husband,” said she, “if I cannot make the sun and the moon rise, and must look on while the sun and the moon rise of themselves, I cannot bear it, and shall not have a quiet hour more, since I cannot make them rise myself.”
sentences.n: kan = High German ‘kann’ (can); kene = ‘keine’ (no); geruhige = ‘ruhige’ (quiet); Stünd = ‘Stunde’ (hour); sülwst = ‘selbst’ (myself).

sentences.t: Do seeg se em so recht gräsig an, dat em so’n Schudder äwerleep.
sentences.l: Then she looked at him so right gruesomely, that such a shudder ran over him.
sentences.i: Then she looked at him so gruesomely that a shudder ran over him.
sentences.n: gräsig = High German ‘gräßlich’ (gruesome, ghastly); Schudder = ‘Schauder’ (shudder); äwerleep = ‘überlief’ (ran over).

sentences.t: „Glyk ga hen, ik will warden as de lewe Gott.“
sentences.l: “Go at once thither, I will become like the dear God.”

sentences.t: „Ach, Fru,“ säd de Mann, un füll vör eer up de Knee, „dat kann de Butt nich.
sentences.l: “Oh, wife,” said the man, and fell before her on his knees, “that the flounder cannot do.
sentences.i: “Oh, wife,” said the man, and fell on his knees before her, “that the flounder cannot do.

sentences.t: Kaiser un Paabst kann he maken, ik bidd dy, sla in dy un blyf Paabst.“
sentences.l: Emperor and pope he can make, I pray you, strike into yourself and stay pope.”
sentences.i: Emperor and pope he can make; I pray you, come to your senses and stay pope.”
sentences.n: ‘sla in dy’ = High German ‘schlag in dich’ (an idiom: come to your senses, relent); sla = ‘schlag’ (strike); blyf = ‘bleib’ (stay).

sentences.t: Do köhm se in de Booshait, de Hoor flögen ehr so wild üm den Kopp, da reet se sik dat Lyfken up, un geef em eens mit dem Foot un schreed „ik holl dat nich uut, un holl dat nich länger uut, wult du hengaan??“
sentences.l: Then she came into a fury, the hair flew so wild about her head, then she tore her bodice open, and gave him one with her foot and shrieked, “I bear it not, and bear it no longer, will you go??”
sentences.i: Then she flew into a rage, her hair flying wild about her head; she tore her bodice open, gave him a kick with her foot, and shrieked, “I cannot bear it, I cannot bear it any longer — will you go??”
sentences.n: Booshait = High German ‘Bosheit’ (fury, malice); Hoor = ‘Haar(e)’ (hair); Kopp = ‘Kopf’ (head); ‘reet ... up’ = ‘riss auf’ (tore open); Lyfken = ‘Leibchen’ (bodice); geef = ‘gab’ (gave); eens = ‘eins’ (one); Foot = ‘Fuß’ (foot); schreed = ‘schrie’ (shrieked); ‘holl ... uut’ = ‘halt aus’ (bear, hold out); länger = ‘länger’ (longer).

sentences.t: Do slööpd he sik de Büxen an un leep wech as unsinnig.
sentences.l: Then he pulled his breeches on and ran away like mad.
sentences.i: Then he pulled on his breeches and ran off like a madman.
sentences.n: ‘slööpd ... an’ = High German ‘schlüpfte an’ (pulled on, slipped on); Büxen = ‘Hosen’ (breeches); leep = ‘lief’ (ran); wech = ‘weg’ (away); unsinnig = ‘unsinnig/wie wahnsinnig’ (like mad).

sentences.t: Buten awer güng de Storm, un bruusde dat he kuum up den Föten staan kunn: de Huser un de Bömer waiden um, un de Baarge beewden, un de Felsenstücken rullden in de See, un de Himmel wöör ganß pickswart, un dat dunnerd un blitzd, un de See güng in so hoge swarte Bülgen as Kirchentöörn un as Baarge, un de hadden bawen all ene witte Kroon von Schuum up.
sentences.l: But outside the storm went, and roared so that he could scarcely stand on his feet: the houses and the trees blew over, and the mountains quaked, and the rock-pieces rolled into the sea, and the sky was all pitch-black, and it thundered and lightened, and the sea heaved in such high black billows as church-towers and as mountains, and they had all a white crown of foam on above.
sentences.i: But outside the storm was raging, and roared so that he could scarcely stand on his feet; the houses and the trees blew over, and the mountains quaked, and rocks rolled into the sea, and the sky was all pitch-black, and it thundered and lightened, and the sea heaved in black billows high as church-towers and as mountains, each crowned above with a white crown of foam.
sentences.n: Buten = High German ‘draußen’ (outside); Storm = ‘Sturm’ (storm); bruusde = ‘brauste’ (roared); kuum = ‘kaum’ (scarcely); Föten = ‘Füßen’ (feet); Huser = ‘Häuser’ (houses); Bömer = ‘Bäume’ (trees); ‘waiden um’ = ‘wehten um’ (blew over); Baarge = ‘Berge’ (mountains); beewden = ‘bebten’ (quaked); Felsenstücken = ‘Felsenstücke’ (rocks); rullden = ‘rollten’ (rolled); pickswart = ‘pechschwarz’ (pitch-black); dunnerd = ‘donnerte’ (thundered); blitzd = ‘blitzte’ (lightened); hoge = ‘hohe’ (high); swarte = ‘schwarze’ (black); Kirchentöörn = ‘Kirchtürme’ (church-towers); bawen = ‘oben’ (above); witte = ‘weiße’ (white); Schuum = ‘Schaum’ (foam).

sentences.t: Do schre he, un kun syn egen Woord nich hören,
sentences.l: Then he cried out, and could not hear his own word,
sentences.i: Then he cried out, and could not hear his own words,
sentences.n: schre = High German ‘schrie’ (cried); kun = ‘konnte’ (could); egen = ‘eigen’ (own); Woord = ‘Wort’ (word).

sentences.t:
„Manntje, Manntje, Timpe Te,
Buttje, Buttje in der See,
myne Fru de Ilsebill
will nich so as ik wol will.“
sentences.l:
“Mannikin, mannikin, Timpe Te,
Flounder, flounder in the sea,
my wife the Ilsebill
will not as I would will.”
sentences.i:
“Little man, little man, Timpe Te,
Flounder, flounder in the sea,
my wife, my Ilsebill,
wills not as I would will.”
sentences.n: The last time the refrain is cried, into the full storm.

sentences.t: „Na, wat will se denn?“ säd de Butt.
sentences.l: “Well, what does she want then?” said the flounder.
sentences.i: “Well, what does she want, then?” said the flounder.

sentences.t: „Ach,“ säd he, „se will warden as de lewe Gott.“
sentences.l: “Oh,” said he, “she wants to become like the dear God.”

sentences.t: „Ga man hen, se sitt all weder in’n Pißputt.“
sentences.l: “Just go thither, she is sitting again in the pisspot already.”
sentences.i: “Just go home, she is sitting again in the hovel.”
sentences.n: sitt = High German ‘sitzt’ (sits); weder = ‘wieder’ (again). And so the last wish undoes everything — they are thrown back into the Pißputt where they began.

sentences.t: Door sitten se noch bet up hüüt un düssen Dag.
sentences.l: There they sit still up to today and this day.
sentences.i: And there they sit to this very day.
sentences.n: sitten = High German ‘sitzen’ (sit); düssen = ‘diesen’ (this).
